answer:
1. Definition & Core Concept

PostgreSQL index bottlenecks occur when indexes that are supposed to speed up queries instead degrade performance: unused indexes waste write I/O (every INSERT/UPDATE maintains all indexes), bloated indexes from dead tuples slow scans, wrong index types (B-tree vs GIN vs GiST) cause full scans, and missing indexes force sequential scans on tables with millions of rows. Instrumenting these requires EXPLAIN ANALYZE, pg_stat_user_indexes, and index bloat monitoring.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System (PostgreSQL/Prisma), the Leave table had indexes on userId, status, createdAt, and startDate. As the table grew to 50k records in testing, Manoj noticed the dashboard query (WHERE status = 'pending' AND userId IN (...)) was doing a sequential scan despite the indexes. EXPLAIN ANALYZE revealed the planner chose a seq scan because the status index had low selectivity (80% of records were 'pending'). Manoj created a partial index: CREATE INDEX idx_pending_leaves ON "Leave"(userId) WHERE status = 'pending' — the index was 90% smaller and the planner used it consistently, dropping query time from 120ms to 8ms.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

-- Identify unused indexes
SELECT schemaname, indexrelname, idx_scan, idx_tup_read,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;

-- Identify bloated indexes
SELECT indexrelname,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size,
       idx_scan AS times_used
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE schemaname = 'public'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;

-- Create partial index for high-selectivity queries
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_pending_leaves
ON "Leave"("userId", "createdAt" DESC)
WHERE status = 'pending';

-- Prisma migration for partial index
// In prisma/migrations/xxx_add_partial_index.sql:
// CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_pending_leaves
// ON "Leave"("userId", "createdAt" DESC)
// WHERE status = 'pending';

// Prisma schema annotation
model Leave {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  userId    String
  status    String   @default("pending")
  startDate DateTime
  endDate   DateTime
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  @@index([userId, createdAt(sort: Desc)])
  // Note: partial index requires raw SQL migration
}

// Monitoring in Fastify
app.get('/api/admin/index-stats', async () => {
  return prisma.$queryRaw`
    SELECT indexrelname, idx_scan, idx_tup_read,
           pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
    FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
    WHERE schemaname = 'public'
    ORDER BY idx_scan ASC
  `;
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

The HR dashboard's "Pending Approvals" page loaded slowly (120ms). Manoj ran EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the query and saw Seq Scan on Leave instead of an index scan. The existing B-tree index on status wasn't being used because 80% of rows had status = 'pending' — PostgreSQL's planner correctly determined a sequential scan was cheaper than an index scan that would return most of the table. The fix: a partial index filtered to only pending leaves, making the index 90% smaller and highly selective. Additionally, Manoj queried pg_stat_user_indexes and found two indexes (on Leave.updatedAt and Leave.type) with zero scans — leftover from early development. Dropping them improved INSERT throughput by 20% because each new leave no longer updated 6 indexes, just 4.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Index on low-selectivity columnPlanner ignores it, wastes write I/OUse partial index or composite index with selective column first
Too many indexes per tableEvery INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE maintains allAudit with pg_stat_user_indexes; drop unused
Never REINDEXIndex bloat from dead tuples; larger scansSchedule REINDEX CONCURRENTLY monthly
B-tree for text searchLIKE '%term%' can't use B-treeUse GIN with pg_trgm for text search
Missing composite indexTwo-column WHERE uses only one indexCreate composite index matching query pattern

7. Validation Strategy

  • EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Every new query must show Index Scan (not Seq Scan) for filtered queries on large tables.
  • Index audit: Monthly check of pg_stat_user_indexes; alert on indexes with 0 scans older than 30 days.
  • Write benchmark: Measure INSERT throughput before/after dropping unused indexes; target 10%+ improvement.
  • Bloat monitor: Track index size vs table size ratio; alert when ratio > 3x.

1. Definition & Core Concept

PostgreSQL Indexes deep dive covers the trade-offs between index types (B-tree, Hash, GIN, GiST, BRIN), the cost of index maintenance on write operations, partial and covering indexes, index-only scans, and failure modes like index corruption, bloat, and planner misestimates. An interviewer-grade answer must explain when each index type is optimal, how the planner decides whether to use an index, and what to do when things go wrong.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System: B-tree on userId for equality lookups. Partial B-tree on status = 'pending' for dashboard filter. Composite B-tree on (userId, createdAt DESC) for "my recent leaves" page. In the CRM Sales Dashboard (MongoDB): equivalent compound indexes on {assigneeId: 1, updatedAt: -1}. Trade-off: each additional index increased write latency by ~0.5ms on the HR system (measured via Prisma middleware logging). Failure mode: during a production migration that added a new index without CONCURRENTLY, the Leave table was locked for 12 seconds — all writes queued behind the lock. Rollback plan: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY if the new index causes performance regression.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

-- Covering index for index-only scans
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_leaves_user_status
ON "Leave"("userId", "status")
INCLUDE ("startDate", "endDate");
-- Query can be satisfied entirely from index (no heap access):
-- SELECT "startDate", "endDate" FROM "Leave"
-- WHERE "userId" = $1 AND status = 'pending'

-- BRIN index for time-series audit log
CREATE INDEX idx_audit_created ON "AuditLog"
USING BRIN("createdAt")
WITH (pages_per_range = 128);
-- Tiny index (1MB vs 50MB B-tree) for time-range queries

-- GIN index for JSONB metadata search
CREATE INDEX idx_leave_metadata ON "Leave"
USING GIN(metadata jsonb_path_ops);
-- Supports: WHERE metadata @> '{"urgent": true}'

-- Prisma: check query plan programmatically
async function analyzeQuery(query: string) {
  const plan = await prisma.$queryRawUnsafe(
    `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, FORMAT JSON) ${query}`
  );
  const node = plan[0]['QUERY PLAN'][0]['Plan'];
  return {
    nodeType: node['Node Type'],  // 'Index Scan' vs 'Seq Scan'
    actualTime: node['Actual Total Time'],
    rowsEstimate: node['Plan Rows'],
    rowsActual: node['Actual Rows'],
  };
}

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

During a Prisma migration on the HR system, Manoj added a new index with @@index([startDate]) in the Prisma schema. The generated migration used CREATE INDEX (without CONCURRENTLY) — this locked the Leave table for 12 seconds during deployment, causing all write requests to queue and return 503. The fix: Manoj learned to use raw SQL migrations for index creation: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY in a separate migration file, which builds the index without locking. He also added a pre-deployment checklist: any migration containing CREATE INDEX must use CONCURRENTLY and be deployed during low-traffic hours.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLYTable locked for writes during buildAlways use CONCURRENTLY in production migrations
B-tree for text search (LIKE '%x%')Index not used, seq scanUse GIN with pg_trgm extension
Covering index missing INCLUDEd columnsExtra heap fetch per rowINCLUDE frequently selected columns in index
Planner misestimate (stale stats)Wrong plan chosen, slow queryANALYZE table after bulk load/delete
Index corruption after crashWrong results or errorsREINDEX CONCURRENTLY; enable checksums

7. Validation Strategy

  • Migration review: CI check rejects migrations containing CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY.
  • Query plan CI: Test suite runs EXPLAIN on critical queries; assert Index Scan not Seq Scan.
  • Index coverage: After deploy, verify new index has > 0 scans within 1 hour via pg_stat_user_indexes.
  • Rollback drill: Practice DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY in staging; measure impact.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Query Plans at scale requires designing PostgreSQL queries whose execution plans remain efficient as data grows from thousands to millions of rows. The query planner uses statistics (row counts, value distributions, correlation) to choose between sequential scans, index scans, bitmap scans, hash joins, merge joins, and nested loops. At 1 M+ rows, a plan that was optimal at 1k rows (nested loop) may become catastrophic — the planner must be guided with proper indexes, up-to-date statistics, and query structure.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, the "leave calendar" view joined Leave, User, and Department tables to show all leaves for a month across the organization. At 50 employees with 1k leaves total, PostgreSQL used a nested loop join (0.5ms). Manoj simulated 1 M leaves and the plan switched to a hash join — but the hash table didn't fit in work_mem (default 4MB), causing a temp file spill to disk (200ms). Increasing work_mem to 64MB for reporting queries (via SET LOCAL) kept the hash in memory (15ms). The architecture: separate connection pool for reporting queries with higher work_mem, while OLTP queries use the default to avoid memory pressure.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Fastify route with optimized query plan for reporting
app.get('/api/reports/leave-calendar', async (req) => {
  const { month, year } = req.query;
  const startDate = new Date(year, month - 1, 1);
  const endDate = new Date(year, month, 0);

  // Use reporting connection with higher work_mem
  const result = await prismaReporting.$queryRaw`
    SET LOCAL work_mem = '64MB';
    SELECT l.id, l."startDate", l."endDate", l.status,
           u.name AS employee, d.name AS department
    FROM "Leave" l
    JOIN "User" u ON l."userId" = u.id
    JOIN "Department" d ON u."departmentId" = d.id
    WHERE l."startDate" >= ${startDate}
      AND l."endDate" <= ${endDate}
    ORDER BY l."startDate"
  `;
  return result;
});

// Automated query plan check in tests
async function assertIndexScan(query: string) {
  const plan = await prisma.$queryRawUnsafe(
    `EXPLAIN (FORMAT JSON) ${query}`
  );
  const nodeType = plan[0]['QUERY PLAN'][0]['Plan']['Node Type'];
  if (nodeType === 'Seq Scan') {
    throw new Error(`Query uses Seq Scan — add index or rewrite: ${query}`);
  }
}

// Statistics freshness check
app.get('/api/admin/stale-stats', async () => {
  return prisma.$queryRaw`
    SELECT relname, last_analyze, last_autoanalyze,
           n_live_tup, n_dead_tup
    FROM pg_stat_user_tables
    WHERE last_analyze < NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days'
       OR last_analyze IS NULL
    ORDER BY n_live_tup DESC
  `;
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

The HR system's monthly leave report took 200ms in production but only 5ms in development. Manoj ran EXPLAIN ANALYZE in both environments and found the production plan used a Hash Join with Batches: 4 (disk spill), while dev used Batches: 1 (in-memory). The difference: production had 50k leaves vs dev's 500. The hash table for the join exceeded the 4MB work_mem default. Rather than increasing work_mem globally (which would multiply by every connection × every sort/hash), Manoj used SET LOCAL work_mem = '64MB' at the start of reporting queries only. He also scheduled ANALYZE after the nightly batch job that processed carry-forward balances, ensuring the planner had fresh statistics.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
work_mem too low for joinsHash/sort spills to disk → 10-50x slowerSET LOCAL work_mem for reporting; separate pool
Stale table statisticsPlanner chooses wrong join strategyANALYZE after bulk operations; monitor last_analyze
Nested loop on large tablesO(n×m) scan, catastrophic at scaleEnsure join columns are indexed; consider hash/merge join
SELECT * with unnecessary columnsWider rows prevent index-only scansSelect only needed columns; use covering indexes
No LIMIT on reporting queriesReturning 1M rows to app memoryAlways paginate; use cursor-based for large exports

7. Validation Strategy

  • Plan regression test: CI runs EXPLAIN on top 10 queries against a production-like dataset; assert no Seq Scan on indexed columns.
  • work_mem test: Load test with 1M rows; verify no temp_blks_written in pg_stat_statements for critical queries.
  • Statistics freshness: Alert when any table's last_analyze is > 24 hours old and n_dead_tup > 10% of n_live_tup.
  • Performance budget: No reporting query > 100ms p99 with production data volume.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Query Plan performance bottlenecks occur when PostgreSQL's planner makes suboptimal choices: choosing nested loops over hash joins for large datasets, using sequential scans when index scans would be faster, or underestimating row counts leading to insufficient memory allocation. Instrumenting these requires EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS), pg_stat_statements for aggregate query metrics, and auto_explain to log slow query plans automatically.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj enabled pg_stat_statements to find the top 5 slowest queries by total time. The #1 offender: the leave overlap detection query (WHERE startDate <= $1 AND endDate >= $2 AND userId = $3 AND status = 'approved') was doing a Bitmap Heap Scan with Recheck Cond — the bitmap exceeded work_mem, forcing a recheck of every row. The fix: a composite index on (userId, status, startDate, endDate) enabled a pure Index Scan, eliminating the bitmap entirely. Query time dropped from 45ms to 3ms. Manoj also enabled auto_explain with log_min_duration = 50ms to automatically capture plans for slow queries in production logs.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

-- Enable pg_stat_statements for query monitoring
-- postgresql.conf: shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_stat_statements;

-- Top 5 slowest queries by total time
SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time::numeric(10,2) AS avg_ms,
       total_exec_time::numeric(10,2) AS total_ms,
       rows, stddev_exec_time::numeric(10,2) AS stddev_ms
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 5;

-- Enable auto_explain for production slow query logging
-- postgresql.conf:
-- shared_preload_libraries = 'auto_explain'
-- auto_explain.log_min_duration = '50ms'
-- auto_explain.log_analyze = true
-- auto_explain.log_buffers = true

-- Composite index for overlap detection
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_leave_overlap
ON "Leave"("userId", status, "startDate", "endDate")
WHERE status = 'approved';

// Prisma middleware to log slow queries
prisma.$use(async (params, next) => {
  const start = performance.now();
  const result = await next(params);
  const duration = performance.now() - start;
  if (duration > 50) {
    console.warn(`Slow query: ${params.model}.${params.action} took ${duration.toFixed(1)}ms`);
  }
  return result;
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

The leave overlap detection — checking if a new leave overlaps with any approved leave — was called on every leave submission. At 50 employees it was invisible (3ms). When Manoj load-tested with 10k leave records, EXPLAIN ANALYZE showed a Bitmap Heap Scan with Rows Removed by Recheck: 8500 — the planner built a bitmap, but it was too large for work_mem, so it had to recheck 85% of the rows against the heap. The composite index on (userId, status, startDate, endDate) with a partial filter WHERE status = 'approved' allowed PostgreSQL to do a precise Index Scan, fetching only the exact matching rows. Manoj added this as a Prisma raw migration and verified with EXPLAIN ANALYZE that the plan now showed Index Scan using idx_leave_overlap.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Bitmap exceeds work_memRecheck condition scans heap → 10x slowerAdd precise composite index; increase work_mem for specific queries
Planner row estimate off by 100xChooses wrong join strategyRun ANALYZE; use extended statistics for correlated columns
No pg_stat_statementsCan't identify top slow queriesEnable as shared_preload_library; review weekly
Not using BUFFERS in EXPLAINCan't see I/O cost (cache vs disk)Always use EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) for investigation
Optimizing wrong queryEffort wasted on infrequent queriesSort by total_exec_time (calls × mean), not just mean

7. Validation Strategy

  • CI plan check: Run EXPLAIN on overlap query with 100k test rows; assert Index Scan, no Recheck Cond.
  • pg_stat_statements review: Weekly top-5 review; any query with mean > 50ms gets investigation ticket.
  • auto_explain alert: Parse auto_explain logs; alert on new Seq Scans appearing on indexed tables.
  • Regression test: After schema change, compare top-10 query plans before/after; flag any plan changes.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Transaction performance bottlenecks arise from lock contention, long-running transactions holding resources, connection pool exhaustion, and the overhead of WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) for durability. At high throughput, transactions that work fine at 10 TPS become bottlenecks at 1000 TPS: locks queue up, connections are held longer, and WAL writes become the I/O bottleneck. Instrumenting requires monitoring pg_stat_activity (active transactions), pg_locks (lock waits), and transaction duration histograms.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, the leave submission flow wrapped 4 operations in a single transaction: (1) validate balance, (2) create leave record, (3) decrement balance, (4) create audit log entry. At peak usage (Monday mornings), 20 concurrent submissions caused connection pool exhaustion — each transaction held a connection for 50-200ms while waiting for the FOR UPDATE lock on the balance row. Manoj instrumented with Prisma's query logging and pg_stat_activity to find that 60% of transaction time was spent waiting for locks. Fix: moved the audit log creation outside the transaction (it doesn't need transactional consistency with the balance update), reducing transaction scope from 4 operations to 3 and average duration from 150ms to 80ms.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Optimized transaction scope in Fastify
app.post('/api/leaves', async (req, reply) => {
  // Only transactional operations inside $transaction
  const leave = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    // 1. Check balance (with lock)
    const balance = await tx.$queryRaw`
      SELECT * FROM "LeaveBalance"
      WHERE "userId" = ${req.user.id}
      FOR UPDATE
    `;
    if (balance[0].remaining < req.body.days) {
      throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
    }

    // 2. Create leave
    const leave = await tx.leave.create({ data: req.body });

    // 3. Decrement balance
    await tx.leaveBalance.update({
      where: { userId: req.user.id },
      data: { remaining: { decrement: req.body.days } },
    });

    return leave;
  }, {
    timeout: 5000,            // Fail fast if lock takes > 5s
    isolationLevel: 'ReadCommitted',
  });

  // 4. Audit log OUTSIDE transaction (eventual consistency OK)
  prisma.auditLog.create({
    data: { action: 'leave_created', leaveId: leave.id, userId: req.user.id },
  }).catch(err => console.error('Audit log failed:', err));

  // 5. Socket.io notification OUTSIDE transaction
  io.to(req.user.managerId).emit('new-leave', leave);

  return reply.status(201).send(leave);
});

// Monitor active transactions
app.get('/api/admin/active-txns', async () => {
  return prisma.$queryRaw`
    SELECT pid, state, query, age(now(), xact_start) AS duration,
           wait_event_type, wait_event
    FROM pg_stat_activity
    WHERE state != 'idle'
    ORDER BY xact_start ASC
  `;
});

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

On a Monday morning, the HR system's API returned 503 errors for ~10 seconds. Manoj checked pg_stat_activity and found 10 active transactions all in active state with wait_event = 'Lock' — they were all waiting for the FOR UPDATE lock on the same user's LeaveBalance row (a department head approving multiple leaves). The connection pool (10 connections) was fully occupied by these waiting transactions, and new requests couldn't get a connection. Fixes: (1) Reduced transaction scope (moved audit log outside). (2) Added a 5-second timeout to $transaction. (3) Increased pool to 20 connections. (4) Added NOWAIT to the FOR UPDATE for the approval endpoint, returning a 409 immediately if the row was locked.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
Side effects inside transactionNotification/audit holds connection longerMove non-critical ops outside transaction
No transaction timeoutStuck transactions hold connections foreverSet idle_in_transaction_session_timeout + app timeout
FOR UPDATE without NOWAITQueue of blocked transactionsNOWAIT or SKIP LOCKED for non-critical operations
synchronous_commit for all writesWAL flush bottleneck at high TPSUse synchronous_commit=off for non-critical writes
Long-running read transactionPrevents VACUUM, table/index bloatSet statement_timeout for read queries

7. Validation Strategy

  • Concurrency test: 20 concurrent leave submissions; assert no pool exhaustion (all complete within 5s).
  • Transaction time: Log and alert on transactions > 1s; p99 target < 500ms.
  • Lock monitoring: pg_locks dashboard; alert when lock wait count > 5 for > 10 seconds.
  • Pool health: Monitor active/idle/waiting connections; alert when utilization > 80%.

1. Definition & Core Concept

Transactions deep dive for interviewer grade covers ACID properties in PostgreSQL, transaction isolation in practice, savepoints for partial rollbacks, two-phase commit for distributed transactions, and the trade-offs between transaction scope (narrow = better concurrency, wide = stronger consistency). Failure modes include: deadlocks, phantom reads, serialization failures, and the "long transaction" problem that blocks autovacuum.

2. Concrete Use-Case from Manoj's Projects

In the HR Leave Management System, the 3-level approval chain used transactions at each level: Level 1 (Team Lead) approval was a transaction that updated leave status + created an approval record. Level 2 (HR Manager) added a check for policy compliance within the same transaction. Level 3 (Director) finalized and triggered the balance deduction. Trade-off: wrapping all 3 levels in one transaction would be the strongest consistency guarantee, but it would mean the Director's approval is blocked if the HR Manager's record is locked. Instead, Manoj used one transaction per level with optimistic locking (version column) between levels. Failure mode: if Level 2 approval crashed mid-transaction, the savepoint allowed partial rollback to the pre-approval state without undoing Level 1. Rollback plan: if transaction timeouts spike, fall back to a queue-based approval flow where each level writes to a job queue.

3. Visual SVG Diagram

4. Code Example

// Transaction with savepoint for partial rollback
async function approveLevelTwo(leaveId: string, managerId: string) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    // Optimistic lock check
    const leave = await tx.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: leaveId } });
    if (leave.version !== 2) {
      throw new Error('Level 1 not completed or already at Level 2');
    }

    // Savepoint: policy check might fail without rolling back status update
    const policyResult = await checkLeavePolicy(tx, leave);
    if (!policyResult.ok) {
      // Partial failure — leave stays at Level 1 status
      await tx.approvalLog.create({
        data: {
          leaveId, managerId,
          action: 'policy_rejected',
          reason: policyResult.reason,
        },
      });
      throw new Error(`Policy violation: ${policyResult.reason}`);
    }

    // Policy passed — approve Level 2
    const updated = await tx.leave.update({
      where: { id: leaveId, version: 2 }, // Optimistic lock
      data: {
        status: 'level2_approved',
        version: { increment: 1 },
      },
    });

    await tx.approval.create({
      data: { leaveId, managerId, level: 2, decision: 'approved' },
    });

    return updated;
  }, {
    timeout: 5000,
    isolationLevel: 'ReadCommitted',
  });
}

// Deadlock detection and retry
async function withRetry(fn: () => Promise, maxRetries = 3): Promise {
  for (let i = 0; i < maxRetries; i++) {
    try {
      return await fn();
    } catch (err: any) {
      if (err.code === '40P01' || err.code === 'P2034') { // Deadlock or serialization
        await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, Math.pow(2, i) * 100));
        continue;
      }
      throw err;
    }
  }
  throw new Error('Max retries exceeded');
}

5. Debugging Narrative from Manoj's Experience

A rare but critical bug: two managers simultaneously approved leaves for employees in each other's departments. Manager A locked Employee X's leave, then tried to check Department Y's policy (which needed a lock on Department Y's config). Manager B did the reverse. PostgreSQL detected the deadlock and rolled back Manager B's transaction with error code 40P01. Manoj's withRetry wrapper caught this and retried after 200ms. To prevent future deadlocks, Manoj enforced a consistent lock ordering: always lock resources in alphabetical order of their IDs within a transaction. This simple rule eliminated deadlocks entirely in subsequent testing.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation Table

PitfallImpact at ScaleMitigation
No transaction timeoutStuck transactions hold connections foreverSet timeout in Prisma $transaction + idle_in_transaction_session_timeout
Deadlocks from inconsistent lock orderingRandom transaction failuresAlways lock resources in consistent order (e.g., by ID)
Retrying without backoffThundering herd after serialization failureExponential backoff with jitter
Wide transactions across domainsLock contention across unrelated tablesOne transaction per aggregate/domain
Ignoring deadlock_timeout configDefault 1s too long for interactive appsSet deadlock_timeout = 200ms for OLTP

7. Validation Strategy

  • Deadlock test: Deliberately create cross-lock scenario; verify detection + retry succeeds.
  • Timeout test: Simulate stuck transaction (pg_sleep); verify Prisma timeout triggers after 5s.
  • Savepoint test: Fail policy check mid-transaction; verify leave status unchanged (rolled back to savepoint).
  • Bloat test: Run long transaction, then check pg_stat_user_tables.n_dead_tup; verify VACUUM runs after txn ends.
